title: When to Move to Fine-Tuning
concept: If a task is highly repetitive and requires maximum speed and consistency, transition from in-context prompting to dedicated fine-tuning. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Instruction Tuning.'

exercises.prompt: When is it most beneficial to transition from prompting to fine-tuning a model?

options.text: For writing a one-off poem for your friend's birthday
For classifying millions of customer reviews daily with high speed and consistency
For asking general trivia questions once a week

exercises.explain: Prompting is great for prototyping, but repetitive, high-speed, and high-volume tasks are where fine-tuning shines.

cards.title: Passing the Prompt Limit
cards.body: Prompt engineering is awesome for testing ideas quickly. But what happens when you need to run the same prompt millions of times a day, as fast and cheap as possible? That's when you cross the bridge to fine-tuning!

cards.title: What is Fine-Tuning?
cards.body: Fine-tuning is the process of taking a pre-trained general AI model and training it further on a specific dataset. Instead of giving it instructions in every single prompt (in-context learning), you permanently bake the behavior, style, and structure directly into the model's 'brain'.

cards.title: The Chef's Recipe Book
cards.body: Imagine trying to cook a complex recipe by constantly reading a massive instruction booklet taped to your hand (prompt engineering). It works, but it's slow. Fine-tuning is like going to culinary school: you train your brain beforehand so you can cook that dish instantly and perfectly without looking at the instructions.

cards.title: From Prompts to Tuning
cards.body: Suppose you run a support bot that classifies customer emails into 10 categories. Instead of sending a giant prompt with 20 examples every single time, you fine-tune a model on 1,000 historical categorized emails. The fine-tuned model does the job instantly with a short, simple prompt.

cards.title: Myth: Tuning is for Facts
cards.body: Many think fine-tuning is the best way to teach a model brand-new knowledge (like a company's private documents). Actually, fine-tuning is best for teaching *behavior, style, and format*. For injecting fresh external facts, search tools and RAG are much better!

cards.title: Comparing the Approaches
cards.body: Let's compare the two approaches for running a high-volume repetitive task.

right.text: Fast, cheap per run, requires training dataset upfront, highly consistent format.

left.text: Slow, expensive for big volumes, easy to setup, requires long instruction text every time.

exercises.prompt: Why does transitioning to a fine-tuned model save on token costs?

options.text: Because it allows you to write longer prompts with more explanations.
Because you don't have to repeat instructions and examples in every prompt.
Because fine-tuned models are completely free to use.
Because it deletes the model's ability to read English.

exercises.explain: Prompts with many examples are slow and use many tokens. Fine-tuning bakes those patterns into the model, keeping prompts short, fast, and cheap.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Setting up a fine-tuned model requires more upfront dataset preparation than simple prompt engineering.
exercises.explain: Fine-tuning requires preparing a high-quality dataset of examples to train the model, which takes more upfront effort than simply writing a prompt.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word about the benefits of fine-tuning.
exercises.sentence: Fine-tuning helps you save costs and increase processing speed because you no longer need giant prompts.
exercises.explain: By removing long instructions and examples from the prompt, the model processes requests much faster and cheaper.

exercises.prompt: Which AI is using prompt engineering (in-context learning) instead of being fine-tuned?

options.text: Input: 'Classify this review as Positive or Negative. Example: I hate it -> Negative. Review: I love this!' -> Output: 'Positive'
Input: 'Review: I love this!' -> Output: 'I am not programmed to classify reviews.'

exercises.explain: AI 2 shows the prompt-heavy approach where you must explain the format every single time. AI 1 is fine-tuned to output the exact structure automatically without instructions.

exercises.prompt: When should you make the leap and transition from prompting a general model to creating a dedicated fine-tuned model?

options.text: When we want the model to learn entirely new facts about yesterday's news
When we need maximum speed, lower costs per prompt, and strict formatting consistency
When we want to write a prompt that is as long and wordy as possible
Only when a model completely refuses to answer any simple questions

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning is the ultimate upgrade when you need extreme speed, low costs, and high consistency on a specific repetitive task. Next up: Instruction Tuning.
